description: The figure presents simulation results of dislocation density distributions and flow behaviors for low-alloy steel under hot compression at 850 °C, comparing two strain rates: 0.01 s⁻¹ (top) and 1 s⁻¹ (bottom). Each panel overlays experimental (solid blue curves) and simulated (magenta circles) true stress–true strain curves, alongside 3D cellular automaton renderings at ε = 0.45 and ε = 0.9. At 0.01 s⁻¹, flow stress rises to a peak then gradually declines toward steady state; embedded 3D maps show dislocation densities evolving from 1×10¹¹ m⁻² to 2.821×10¹⁴ m⁻² at ε = 0.45 and to 3.872×10¹⁴ m⁻² at ε = 0.9, with preferential high-density regions along grain boundaries and lower densities inside dynamically recrystallized (DRX) grains. At 1 s⁻¹, the stress level is markedly higher; dislocation densities reach 1.087×10¹⁵ m⁻² at ε = 0.45 and 1.198×10¹⁵ m⁻² at ε = 0.9, indicating suppressed DRX nucleation and growth. The figure supports that DRX grains accumulate lower dislocation density than the matrix, causing flow softening, and that higher strain rates exacerbate microstructural heterogeneity by leaving more unrecrystallized matrix on the 3D model surfaces.
